1: Hosea prophesied in the northern kingdom of Israel ca. 755-725 BCE, the final decades before Assyria destroyed Samaria (722 BCE). His marriage to Gomer — a woman who proved unfaithful — is the enacted metaphor of YHWH's relationship with Israel: YHWH's faithful covenant love (hesed) in the face of Israel's persistent idolatry (harlotry). The marriage metaphor for the YHWH-Israel covenant (developed also in Jeremiah 2-3, Ezekiel 16, and Isaiah 54) is Hosea's central theological contribution: covenant violation is not merely law-breaking but adultery, a betrayal of the intimate covenant-love relationship. The NT develops the bridegroom-bride metaphor for Christ-church (Ephesians 5:25-32; Revelation 19:7-9) directly in line with Hosea's framework.
2: Go and take a wife of unfaithfulness (ʾeshet zenunim) — the opening command is the most striking in the prophetic corpus: YHWH orders Hosea to marry a woman whose character or subsequent behavior would enact the central metaphor of the book. Scholars debate whether Gomer was already a cult prostitute, a woman with a promiscuous past, or simply someone who would prove unfaithful. The phrase zenunim (whoredom, unfaithfulness) is used throughout Hosea as the paradigm of covenant betrayal: Israel’s worship of the Baals is “playing the whore after other gods” (Judges 2:17; Hosea 8:27). The command is simultaneously a personal commission and a prophetic sign-act — Hosea’s marriage is the enacted word of YHWH about his own covenant relationship with Israel.
3: Gomer daughter of Diblaim — the name Gomer may have Phoenician or Canaanite associations (a Gomer appears in the Table of Nations, Genesis 10:2-3, as a northern people). Diblaim may mean “two fig cakes,” possibly pointing to the fertile plenty of Canaan that Israel preferred to YHWH. The historical specificity — a named woman with a named father — grounds the prophetic sign in real biography rather than allegory, lending the message its emotional and rhetorical power. Hosea’s willingness to obey the command despite its social cost embodies the costly love he is called to preach.
4: Name him Jezreel — Jezreel (yizreʿel, “God plants”) is freighted with historical meaning: it was the site of Jehu’s bloody purge of the house of Ahab and the Baal worshipers (2 Kgs 9–10). YHWH had commissioned that purge through Elisha, yet now YHWH announces he will punish the house of Jehu for the bloodshed at Jezreel. The dynasty that came to power through divinely-sanctioned violence is now itself subject to judgment for the manner in which it carried out that violence. The great plain of Jezreel (also called Esdraelon) was the breadbasket of the northern kingdom and a major military theater — its significance here is both military and theological.
5: I will break the bow of Israel in the valley of Jezreel — the bow (qeshet) is the primary weapon of the army; “breaking the bow” = military defeat (cf. Psalms 46:9; Jeremiah 49:35). The valley of Jezreel was the natural corridor through which armies moved into the highlands; control of the valley was control of the north. This oracle was fulfilled when Tiglath-pileser III of Assyria invaded and annexed significant parts of the northern kingdom in 733–732 BCE (2 Kings 15:29), devastating the Jezreel region.
6: Lo-ruhamah — “Not Pitied” or “Not Shown Mercy” (lo ruhamah); the root racham denotes the deep maternal compassion associated with the womb (rechem). YHWH’s withdrawal of rachamim from Israel is the withdrawal of the most fundamental divine tenderness. The distinction between Israel (“no longer show mercy”) and Judah (“I will show mercy,” Hosea 1:7) reflects the historical reality that the northern kingdom faced Assyrian destruction while Jerusalem was spared. The name given to Hosea’s daughter is simultaneously a theological verdict and a prophetic sign lived out in the prophet’s own family.
7: I will save them by the LORD their God; I will not save them by bow, sword, war, horses, or horsemen — this oracle anticipates the miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib’s siege in 701 BCE, when the Assyrian army withdrew without capturing the city after a plague struck the camp (2 Kings 19:35–36; Isaiah 37:36). The explicit disclaimer of military means — bow, sword, war, horses — echoes Deuteronomy’s warning against multiplying horses (Deuteronomy 17:16) and the Psalter’s insistence that “a horse is a vain hope for salvation” (Psalms 33:17). Judah’s deliverance will be YHWH’s act, not human military power.
8: After she had weaned Lo-ruhamah — weaning in the ancient Near East typically occurred between two and three years of age (cf. 2 Macc Hosea 7:27; 1 Samuel 1:23-24). The sequential births mark the progressive deterioration of the covenant relationship: each child’s name carries a darker theological verdict. The time gaps between births create a sense of inexorable movement toward the final, most devastating oracle of Lo-ammi.
9: Lo-ammi — Not My People — the most devastating of the three name-oracles: lo-ammi is the direct reversal of the Sinai covenant formula “you shall be my people and I will be your God” (Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 11:4). YHWH is annulling the covenant in the most explicit way possible: “you are not my people, and I am not your God” (or possibly “I am not ‘I AM’ to you” — lo ehyeh lakhem, using the divine name Ehyeh, “I AM,” from Exodus 3:14). The covenant dissolution is expressed through the naming of a child — the most personal and permanent of acts.
10: The number of the children of Israel will be like the sand of the sea — the reversal of Lo-ammi is expressed in the language of the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 22:17: “I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars of heaven and as the sand that is on the seashore”). The covenant announced to Abraham is not abolished — even the dissolution of the Mosaic covenant does not undo the Abrahamic promise. In the place where it was said to them ‘You are not my people,’ it will be said to them ‘Children of the living God’ — the transformation is geographical and social: in the very location of rejection comes the word of adoption. Paul reads this as applying to both restored Israel and Gentile inclusion (Romans 9:25-26).
11: The children of Judah and Israel will be gathered together and appoint for themselves one leader — the eschatological reunification of the divided monarchy under one king reverses the schism of Jeroboam I (c. 930 BCE; 1 Kgs 12). Great will be the day of Jezreel — the site of judgment (Hosea 1:4-5) becomes the site of blessing: yizreʿel = “God plants” in its positive valence, the great day when YHWH plants his reunited people in the land. The eschatological reversal uses the same place-name for both judgment (the Jehu dynasty falls at Jezreel) and hope (YHWH plants his people at Jezreel).
